ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken karaage bowl; closest_parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002372) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for chicken karaage bowl as a composite dish; A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible parent for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken karaage bowl or equivalent Japanese fried chicken rice bowl concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice topped with 180–200 g of deep-fried chicken karaage pieces and garnishes such as shredded cabbage, mayonnaise, and lemon wedge.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken karaage (deep-fried marinated chicken) with steamed white rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Chicken thigh, cooked, fried (FDC ID 331960) and White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756), weighted by typical bowl composition (approx. 44% rice, 44% fried chicken, 12% garnish/sauce).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken karaage bowl as a composite dish. Values estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for deep-fried chicken thigh (FDC ID 331960: ~211 kcal, 19.8 g protein, 13.5 g fat per 100 g) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756: ~130 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 28.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g), combined at approximately 44% rice, 44% fried chicken, 12% garnish/sauce/coating. Composite per-100 g values calculated accordingly. HPB Singapore Foodo and ICMR-NIN do not carry a direct karaage bowl entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Japanese-style) GI ~50–54; composite bowl GI estimated at 50 weighted by rice fraction (~44% of bowl weight contributing ~28 g carbohydrate per 100 g rice component).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken karaage bowl. GI estimated at 50 based on the dominant glycemic driver being Japanese-style steamed white rice (GI ~50–54 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The fried chicken and garnish components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Composite GI conservatively set to 50 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (50 × 71.1) / 100 ≈ 35.6, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving ≈ 71.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium (~60% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from soy sauce marinade; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a glycemic load of approximately 35.6 per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion to lower postprandial glucose response, and pair with the protein-rich karaage to moderate glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from deep-frying and soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~8.1 g per serving) and the soy sauce marinade adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider grilled chicken alternatives to reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Karaage Bowl (Tori no Karaage Don / 鶏の唐揚げ丼) is a popular Japanese donburi dish consisting of deep-fried marinated chicken pieces served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The chicken, typically thigh meat, is marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, sake, mirin, ginger, and garlic before being coated in potato starch or a light flour-starch blend and deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy inside. The bowl is commonly garnished with shredded cabbage, Japanese mayonnaise, a lemon wedge, and sometimes pickled vegetables or a soft-boiled egg. Karaage is one of Japan's most beloved comfort foods and has become widely popular across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it is served in Japanese restaurant chains and food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 774 kcal, 51.8 g protein, 32.4 g fat, and 71.1 g carbohydrate. The dish is calorie-dense due to deep-frying and the rice base. Sodium content is elevated (~1395 mg per serving) from soy sauce marinade, making it a moderate concern for hypertension management. The glycemic load is moderate-to-high (~35.6) driven by the white rice component, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The protein content is substantial, supporting satiety. Clinically, the dish is best consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease, with portion control and ingredient substitutions recommended.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken karaage bowl?
What is the difference between karaage and tempura?
Is chicken karaage bowl suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in karaage don?
What rice is used in karaage don?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken karaage bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for deep-fried chicken thigh and cooked white rice at typical bowl proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese-style white rice as the dominant glycemic driver. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (FDC ID 331960 for fried chicken thigh; FDC ID 169756 for cooked white rice) weighted at approximately 44% rice, 44% fried chicken, 12% garnish/sauce/coating. No direct approved-source entry for chicken karaage bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. GI set to 50 (low category) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style steamed white rice (GI 50–54); no direct GI measurement for chicken karaage bowl exists in approved GI sources. 4. The food_name 'Chicken Karaage Bowl' is used as the canonical English name; the input menu name '506D Chicken Karaage Bowl (D)' contains a restaurant-specific item code and size designator that are not part of the canonical food identity. 5. Halal status is marked false as karaage traditionally uses sake (rice wine) in the marinade; restaurant-specific halal certification would need to be verified independently. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (50 × 71.1) / 100 = 35.6, where 71.1 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving is derived from 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4.5.
